Vikram Sarabhai

# Vikram Sarabhai

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

![vs](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TCCx!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F21df6cea-6c65-47c9-a74f-1617b8e476b5_353x478.jpeg "vs")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Two students in a room, very pensive, and nervous at their head coming inside. An electric meter had just gone out of order, as they had passed too much current while doing an experiment. And they were wondering how to break the news to him, who was also the founder of the rather modest lab, that had begun a year back. It was a time,when such electric meters were difficult to get, and important experiments could be suspended for a month or even more. As the students nervously broke the news, the man displayed neither the slightest trace of irritation or anger. Instead in a rather comforting tone he told them.

> *“Is that all? Don’t mind it too much. Such things do happen when
> students are learning. If students don’t make mistakes, how can they
> learn? It is enough if you learn to be more careful in future.”*

The man here was none other than Vikram Sarabhai, the father of India’s space program, regarded as one of the greatest scientists of modern India. And who also made a signficant contribution to the nuclear program.

In the heart of Ahmedabad, Vikram Sarabhai entered the world on August 12, 1919, on the auspicious occasion of Garuda Panchami,into a family of wealth, and vision. His father, Ambalal Sarabhai, a renowned textile magnate, and mother Sarala Devi, believed that true education must nurture both intellect and imagination.

Unimpressed by conventional schooling, the Sarabhais built a sanctuary of learning within their home. They invited masterful educators in science and the arts, crafting a curriculum that danced between logic and lyricism. Vikram and his eight siblings thrived in this cocoon of curiosity, learning not just to answer questions, but to ask better ones.

This early foundation—rooted in freedom, rigor, and creativity—shaped Vikram into the father of India’s space program. But before rockets and research centers, there was a boy who studied under mango trees, debated with poets, and dreamed beyond the stars.

The Sarabhai home in Ahmedabad wasn’t just a residence—it was a salon of ideas, a sanctuary for revolutionaries, poets, and philosophers. Under its roof, young Vikram listened as **Gurudev Tagore** recited verses, **C.V. Raman** spoke of light, and **Mahatma Gandhi** healed in silence.

Imagine growing up where **Jawaharlal Nehru** debated policy over tea, **Sarojini Naidu** sang of freedom, and **J. Krishnamurti** questioned the nature of thought itself. These weren’t distant names in textbooks—they were voices that echoed through the corridors of Vikram’s childhood.

Such encounters didn’t just inspire Vikram—they sculpted him. His mind absorbed the rigor of science and the soul of philosophy. He learned that progress wasn’t just about rockets—it was about people, purpose, and possibility.

Brilliance wasn’t just a trait—it was Vikram Sarabhai’s rhythm. A prodigy in mathematics and science, he soared through his studies, always at the top. In 1939, he conquered the **Tripos in Physics** at Cambridge—one of the most formidable academic challenges of its time.

But Vikram’s journey wasn’t about staying abroad—it was about bringing knowledge home. Back in India, he joined the **Indian Institute of Science**, where the legendary **C.V. Raman** led the Physics Department. Side by side with **Dr. Homi Bhabha**, Vikram stepped into a golden era of Indian science.

**Cosmic Rays**

Typically every substance on earth has 3 fundamental particles- the electrons( -ve charge), protons(+ve charge) and neutrons( neutral). However it has been discovered that there are other particles in space, beside these, called as mesons. And these mesons are formed by cosmic rays as per most scientific theories. Over 600 such cosmic rays pass through the human body every year, and they can penetrate the hardest rocks too.

Vikram Sarabhai’s first scientific paper wasn’t just data—it was a doorway. His research on the **periodic variation of cosmic ray intensity** opened up vast questions, How does the sun speak to the earth?, What magnetic secrets lie beneath our feet? and What stories do cosmic rays carry from interplanetary space?

In 1943, Vikram journeyed to the **Himalayas in Kashmir**, seeking answers at high altitudes. Amid the silence of snow and sky, he had a revelation:

“To study the cosmos, we must rise to meet it.”

There, among the clouds, the seed of a **cosmic ray research center** was planted. Not in the comfort of cities, but in the raw embrace of nature—where science could breathe, and the universe could whisper back.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Tkxe!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa27067c6-4934-43d2-aba7-a215555a87ce_640x411.jpeg)

![alpf](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Tkxe!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa27067c6-4934-43d2-aba7-a215555a87ce_640x411.jpeg "alpf")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Long before satellites and launchpads, Vikram Sarabhai stood by the serene **Alpathari Lake**, nestled in **Apharwat** at 13,000 feet. It was a cherished family retreat—where laughter echoed off snowy peaks and summers bloomed in Himalayan silence.

But Vikram saw more than beauty. He saw potential. The thin air, the altitude, the magnetic isolation—it was perfect for studying **cosmic rays**.

In 1945, after WWII, Vikram returned to **Cambridge** to deepen his research and earn his **PhD**. But his heart remained in Apharwat. The lake, the altitude, the dream—they stayed with him, guiding him toward the creation of a **high-altitude research institute**.

In **1948**, Vikram Sarabhai founded the **Physical Research Laboratory (PRL)** in Ahmedabad—a modest beginning with a handful of students and lab assistants. With **Dr. K.R. Ramanathan** as its first director, PRL became the cradle of India’s space science.

Vikram never forgot the heights of Apharwat. In **1955**, PRL sponsored a **cosmic ray research institute in Gulmarg**, bringing his Himalayan vision to life.  
Later, the **Department of Atomic Energy (DAE)** established a full-fledged **high-altitude center** there—his dream, now a reality.

  
The legacy didn’t stop at Gulmarg. Similar centers blossomed in **Kodaikanal** and **Thiruvananthapuram**, mapping India’s scientific ambition across its diverse geography.

  
Even as Vikram’s responsibilities grew, he remained deeply connected to PRL—first as **Professor**, then as **Director in 1965**. His presence was more than administrative—it was spiritual, visionary, unwavering.

In **1966**, India mourned the sudden loss of **Dr. Homi Jehangir Bhabha**—the architect of its nuclear program. The void was immense. Who could carry forward such a legacy?

**Vikram Sarabhai** stepped in—not with grand declarations, but with quiet determination. He took charge of the **Atomic Energy Commission (AEC)**, guiding India’s nuclear ambitions with clarity, caution, and vision.

Under Vikram’s leadership, India’s fledgling nuclear program found direction. He balanced scientific rigor with ethical foresight, ensuring that atomic energy served development—not destruction. He didn’t seek the spotlight. He simply did the work. And in doing so, he proved that leadership isn’t about replacing greatness—it’s about continuing it with integrity.

While the world raced to plant flags on the moon, **Vikram Sarabhai** had a different dream for India. He believed space wasn’t just about exploration—it was about **empowerment**. He persuaded **Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru** that India needed its own space program—not for prestige, but for purpose.

In **1962**, Nehru established **INCOSPAR** (Indian National Committee for Space Research) on Vikram’s recommendation. By **1969**, it evolved into **ISRO**—India’s gateway to the stars, built not on ambition alone, but on relevance.

Vikram knew India couldn’t afford moon missions. But he saw immense value in **satellite TV**, **earth mapping**, and **remote sensing**—tools that could transform agriculture, education, and disaster management. His vision made ISRO not just a scientific institution, but a **social mission**—a beacon of how technology can serve humanity.

This is what he had to say about India’s space program.

> *There are some who question the relevance of space activities in a
> developing nation. To us, there is no ambiguity of purpose. We do not
> have the fantasy of competing with the economically advanced nations
> in the exploration of the Moon or the planets or manned space-flight.
> But we are convinced that if we are to play a meaningful role
> nationally, and in the community of nations, we must be second to none
> in the application of advanced technologies to the real problems of
> man and society.*

In the quiet coastal town of **Thumba**, near **Thiruvananthapuram**, Vikram Sarabhai saw more than palm trees and ocean breeze—he saw potential. Nestled close to the **Earth’s magnetic equator**, Thumba was the perfect site for **atmospheric research** and rocket launches.

With strong support from **Dr. Homi Bhabha**, Vikram established **India’s first space station** here. It wasn’t just a scientific decision—it was a strategic masterstroke. Thumba’s location allowed scientists to study **geomagnetic phenomena**, **ionospheric conditions**, and **cosmic rays** with unmatched precision.

Nov 21, 1963

From the humble launchpad at **Thumba**, the first rocket pierced the sky. It wasn’t just a technical feat—it was the moment **Vikram Sarabhai’s dream took flight.** Among those present was a young engineer named **A.P.J. Abdul Kalam**—wide-eyed, inspired, and destined to become a legend himself.

*“That day, I saw not just a rocket rise, but a nation begin its ascent.”*

In **1966**, Vikram’s collaboration with **NASA** led to **SITE (Satellite Instructional Television Experiment)**—a revolutionary idea to beam education into India’s remotest villages. Space wasn’t just for scientists—it was for **students, farmers, and dreamers**.

The project for India’s first satellite began under Sarabhai’s guidance. Though he didn’t live to see it launch, **Aryabhatta (1975)** was his vision realized—a tribute to his belief in **technology with purpose**. Sarabhai’s journey wasn’t about reaching the stars—it was about **bringing their light home**.

Vikram Sarabhai didn’t just launch satellites—he launched **institutions**, **ideas**, and **inspiration** across every corner of Indian life.

**IIM Ahmedabad, result of a** dream to create a **world-class management institute** in India. Today, it stands as a global beacon of leadership and innovation.

He understood the power of data long before it became a buzzword. **Operations Research Group (ORG)** brought analytical thinking to Indian business.

To support Ahmedabad’s booming textile industry, he founded the **Ahmedabad Textile Industry's Research Association**, blending tradition with technology.

  
Science wasn’t just for labs—it was for **children, teachers, and curious minds**. The **Sarabhai Community Science Center** made science **accessible, playful, and profound**.

With his wife **Mrinalini Sarabhai**, he nurtured India’s cultural soul. Dance, drama, and music found a home where **art met activism**.

He didn’t just dream of rockets—he laid the foundation for **India’s atomic energy and advanced research ecosystem**, brick by brick, lab by lab.

  
**Kalpakkam** **Fast Breeder Test Reactor a** cornerstone in India’s nuclear energy program, enabling **efficient fuel utilization** and **energy independence**.

  
The **Variable Energy Cyclotron Project at Kolkata a** hub for **particle physics research**, this project opened new frontiers in **nuclear science and accelerator technology**.

  
To ensure India’s **uranium self-sufficiency**, Sarabhai helped establish **Uranium Corporation of India Ltd** for **mining and processing nuclear fuel**.

While others slept, **Vikram Sarabhai** stayed in the lab—tweaking circuits, scribbling equations, listening to the quiet hum of possibility. He wasn’t just a leader; he was **hands-on**, sleeves rolled up, solving problems one experiment at a time.

To his students, he wasn’t a distant figure—he was a **guide**, a **dialogue partner**, a **co-dreamer**. He believed that education wasn’t about instruction—it was about **conversation**, **curiosity**, and **collaboration**.

For Vikram, science wasn’t a luxury—it was a **necessity for nation-building**. Every project, every paper, every satellite was aimed at **uplifting India**—its villages, its youth, its future.

Despite his towering achievements, he remained **down-to-earth**, approachable, and kind.

“True greatness,” he believed, “lies in service, not stature.”

Even as rockets soared and institutions rose, **Vikram Sarabhai** never lost sight of what mattered most—**his family**, his roots, and the people around him. In between meetings with ministers and midnight lab sessions, he found time for **family dinners**, **business decisions**, and **quiet conversations**.

  
He didn’t just manage time—he **made space for people**. Colleagues, students, workers, friends—they all came to him. Not just with ideas, but with **worries, hopes, and heartaches**. And Vikram listened—patiently, kindly, without judgment.

When some one asked why he is wasting time listening to all that, this was his reply

> *In our vast land people come from many backgrounds. Not every one is
> lucky enough to have the education we have. So, we have to listen to
> everything they say to understand what is in their mind.*

Vikram Sarabhai walked gently through the world—his mind reaching the stars, his heart rooted in humility.He treated **everyone as equal**—from lab assistants to ministers, from villagers to visionaries.

People came to him not just for guidance, but for comfort. He listened without judgment, helped without hesitation, and uplifted without expectation.In a newly independent India, where class lines still lingered, Vikram quietly erased them—**one conversation, one act of kindness at a time**. He didn’t wear his achievements like medals. He wore them like responsibilities—to serve, to share, to simplify.

On a quiet visit to **Thumba**, December 30, 1971, the birthplace of India’s space program, **Vikram Sarabhai** passed away suddenly from a heart attack. The nation lost a visionary. The cosmos, perhaps, gained one.

He left not from a podium, but from the field—from the very soil where rockets rose and dreams were born. Though Vikram Sarabhai was no more, his spirit lived on in every launch, every lecture, every lab.

Vikram Sarabhai’s legacy is not just in what he built—but in **who he empowered**. From satellites to stage lights, from equations to expressions—he believed in the **limitless potential of the Indian mind**.
